text: This video will demonstrate an XI robotic ETEP incisional hernia repair with mesh. This is a middle-aged male with a BMI of thirty-one. His past medical history is significant for an epigastric ventral hernia repair with mesh remotely and a recent laparoscopic cholecystectomy. He has a recurrent incisional hernia that is seven centimeters wide. The erectus width is about nine centimeters wide. For ETEP, the erectus width of six centimeters or greater is best. The patient also has an umbilical hernia that measures five point three centimeters wide. The combined length of the hernias is nine point five centimeters long.

The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe placed transversely to identify the rectus abdominis muscle, scanning side to side to locate the linea semilunaris and linea alba. These anatomical landmarks are marked on the skin for precise placement of three 8mm robotic ports laterally, just medial to the linea semilunaris.

The surgeon performs entry into the left rectus space using a 5mm optical trocar, identifying the anterior rectus sheath and rectus muscle. Insufflation at 15mmHg pressure creates a CO2 cushion in the retro-rectus space. The trocar is carefully advanced into the space between the fat and posterior sheath, followed by dissection with the obturator. After inserting the scope, additional space is created for a second trocar placement, approximately 6cm from the initial port. A needle is used to identify the track, and further dissection with a Maryland allows for a third port placement in the left lower quadrant. The initial port is upsized to an 8mm robotic port.

The robotic system is docked from the patient's left side at a forty-five degree angle. The surgeon uses scissors in the right hand and a force bipolar in the left hand to dissect the ipsilateral retrorectus space, staying close to the rectus muscle and pulling down the transversalis fascia in the lower abdomen below the arcuate line. After completing stage one, the surgeon prepares to crossover into the preperitoneal space.

The surgeon uses cold cut scissors to initiate the crossover dissection, carefully avoiding the bowel. The dissection reaches the area of the hernia sac, focusing on the posterior sheath. The peritoneum appears translucent, and the goal is to remain extraperitoneal. The surgeon dissects across to the right rectus muscle, reserving the crossover until the preperitoneal dissection is complete. The posterior sheath is opened about a centimeter from the linea alba, where the hernia is located, with caution due to the thin peritoneum and absence of preperitoneal fat.

Below the arcuate line, the surgeon identifies and cuts the left transversalis fascia using scissors, entering the preperitoneal space. The preperitoneal fat is peeled down to reveal the right transversalis fascia. Upon opening the right transversalis fascia, the rectus muscle becomes visible. The surgeon continues dissection of the preperitoneal tissue, aiming to stay anterior to the peritoneum and hernia sac, while addressing the hernia area.

The surgeon identifies the arcuate line and proceeds to dissect the posterior sheath, aiming to take down the hernia sac. A small piece of proline is visible, making the sac sticky during dissection. The surgeon carefully uses scissors and cautery to avoid bleeding while staying outside the peritoneal cavity, occasionally entering the subcutaneous space to preserve the posterior layer's integrity.

The surgeon incises the contralateral posterior sheath about a centimeter away from the linea alba near the umbilicus. The assistant inserts a finger into the umbilicus to prevent accidental cutting across the umbilical stalk. The division of the umbilical stalk from the hernia sac is completed. The surgeon then enters the peritoneal cavity, which facilitates the takedown of the hernia sac by providing a clearer view of its boundaries.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the hernia sac and proceeds to dissect on the opposite side of the neck of the sac. The retrorectus space is accessed from left to right, using a grasper and scissors to open the space. The surgeon enters the peritoneal cavity, taking down omental adhesions within the hernia sac, and uses cautery aggressively due to the presence of mostly fat. A small peritoneal tear is visible and will be closed later with a V-lock suture.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the preperitoneal space past the linea alba and continues to open the posterior sheath. The left-hand grasper checks the position of the linea alba to prevent accidental incision. The posterior sheath is opened well above the incisional hernia site for adequate mesh overlap. The surgeon performs further dissection on the ipsilateral side, cutting more of the ipsilateral posterior sheath. The flex joint is adjusted to increase the range of motion in the right hand, allowing better access to the left upper quadrant. The posterior sheath incision extends to the xiphoid, and the right retrorectal space is developed, with small neurovascular bundles divided and larger ones preserved.

The surgeon completes the retro-rectus dissection, marking the end of the three stages of dissection. The posterior defect is examined to ensure it can be closed without component separation. The surgeon begins closure of the anterior fascia using an OV-Lock suture, carefully grabbing the umbilical stock and some subcutaneous tissue to reduce dead space and seroma size. Two stitches are placed to close the defect, with tension applied to the suture before moving to the next stitch.

The surgeon places multiple sutures along the length of the incision, redistributing tension akin to tightening a corset. Sequential tightening of each suture allows for additional throws as more suture length is gained. The linea alba is re-approximated, addressing a seven-centimeter wide diastasis, with closure extending up to four centimeters below the xiphoid.

The surgeon completes the closure of the defect by running the suture backwards for reinforcement, ensuring the suture line is locked in place. Mesh is placed to cover the area, extending three to four centimeters beyond the diastasis closure for additional support.

The surgeon closes the posterior peritoneal defect with running sutures. The floor space, measuring seventeen centimeters wide and thirty-two centimeters long, is prepared for mesh placement. A piece of parietine mesh is cut to size and inserted through an eight-millimeter port without securing it, as it will be sandwiched between tissue layers. No drains are used for straightforward ETEPs.
